content:
1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
10	    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
11	    present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
12	    multi-route coverage.
13	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
14	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
15	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
16	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
17	    what the directive lists.
18	  - **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` or ``\mathlibok``
19	    markers** (no "after writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no
20	    "verification: confirm `\leanok` is present"). The writer's
21	    descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
22	    deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review
23	    agent. A directive that asks the writer to add markers puts it in
24	    a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the
25	    "verification" instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok
26	    runs. Leave marker concerns out of the writer's directive entirely.
27	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
28	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
29	    writer with TWO globs:
30	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
31	      --write-domain 'references/**'
32	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
33	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
34	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
35	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
36	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
37	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
38	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
39	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
40	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
41	  - After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
42	    in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
43	    confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
44	    the writer fixed everything.
45	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
46	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
47	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
48	    strategy-level issue.
49	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
50	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
51	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
52	---
53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
107	\end{theorem}
108	
109	\begin{proof}
110	  \uses{thm:another_result}
111	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
112	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
113	\end{proof}
114	```
115	
116	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
117	
118	## Rules
119	
120	### What you CAN do
121	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
122	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
123	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
124	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
125	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
126	
127	### What you MUST do
128	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
129	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
130	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
131	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
132	- **Document every change** in your report.
133	
134	### What you MUST NOT do
135	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
136	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
137	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
138	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
139	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
140	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
141	
142	## Reading references
143	
144	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
145	
146	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
147	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
148	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
149	
150	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
151	
152	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
153	
154	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
155	
156	Conditions for dispatch:
157	
158	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
159	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
160	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
161	
162	Dispatch (Bash, foreground, in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
163	
164	```
165	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
166	  --name reference-retriever \
167	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
168	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
169	  --write-domain 'references/**'
170	```
171	
172	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
173	
174	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
175	
176	## Workflow
177	
178	1. Read your directive completely.
179	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references.
180	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
181	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow.
182	5. **If a needed source is missing from `references/`, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and wait for it to return before drafting the affected sections.
183	6. Make the edits.
184	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
185	8. Write your report.
186	
187	## Logging
188	
189	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
190	
191	```markdown
192	# Blueprint Writer Report
193	
194	## Slug
195	<slug>
196	
197	## Status
198	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
199	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
200	
201	## Target chapter
202	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
203	
204	## Changes Made
205	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
206	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
207	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
208	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
209	- ...
210	
211	## Cross-references introduced
212	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
213	- ...
214	
215	## Macros needed (if any)
216	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
217	
218	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
219	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
220	- ...
221	
222	## Notes for Plan Agent
223	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
224	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
225	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
226	
227	## Strategy-modifying findings
228	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
229	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
230	Examples:
231	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
232	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
233	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
234	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
235	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
236	  setup than the strategy assumes.
237	
238	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
239	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
240	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
241	```
242	
243	## Return Value
244	
245	Your final assistant message:
246	
247	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
248	- The path to your full report.
249	
250	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
251

File content (26636 tokens) exceeds maximum allowed tokens (25000). Use offset and limit parameters to read specific portions of the file, or search for specific content instead of reading the whole file.

280	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product $C \times_k T$ and the descent of the abelian-group structure on $\Pic$ along $\pi^*$. The pieces are present individually but not assembled.
281	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$, which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels).
282	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction $G^0 \subseteq G$, and the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$.
283	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form.
284	  \end{itemize}
285	
286	  \paragraph{Route B --- Symmetric powers and Stein factorisation (historical alternative; not pursued).}
287	  \textbf{Iter-144 disposition.} Route B is a historical alternative \emph{not pursued} by the project. It is preserved here for scholarly context only. Route A --- Picard scheme via FGA --- is the committed M3 route per STRATEGY.md \S~M3 iter-144 disposition. The decomposition, gating Mathlib pieces, and midpoint LOC figure ($\sim 9000$ LOC) recorded below are the iter-123 audit values (\texttt{analogies/m3-route-audit.md}); the chapter does not re-cost Route B under current Mathlib snapshots.
288	
289	  This route follows Milne's \emph{Abelian Varieties} (Chapter~III) and avoids FGA representability at the cost of requiring quotients of schemes by finite group actions.
290	
291	  \begin{itemize}
292	    \item[\textbf{(B.1)}] \emph{The $g$-fold symmetric power.} Form the product $C^g$ and let $S_g$ act by permutation of the factors. The categorical quotient $C^{(g)} := C^g / S_g$ exists as a $k$-scheme, smooth and proper over $k$ of dimension $g$. Smoothness follows because the action of $S_g$ on the smooth $k$-scheme $C^g$ is free outside the diagonal locus and the local-ring computation at the diagonal points produces a smooth ring (this is the symmetric-product computation of Fogarty / Mumford). Properness descends from properness of $C^g$ via the geometric-quotient property.
293	
294	    \item[\textbf{(B.2)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi morphism on symmetric powers.} Fix a $k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(k)$. The map
295	    \[
296	      \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
297	      \sum_{i=1}^g [Q_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g Q_i\bigr)
298	    \]
299	    is well-defined (the right-hand side is symmetric in the $Q_i$ and depends only on the unordered tuple) and is surjective. By Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, $\sigma$ is birational onto its image: a generic divisor of degree $g$ on $C$ has $h^0(\mathcal O_C(D)) = 1$, so the fibre $\sigma^{-1}([\mathcal O_C(D)])$ is a point at generic divisors; the special fibres are projective spaces $\mathbb P(h^0 - 1)$ over the locus where $h^0 > 1$.
300	
301	    \item[\textbf{(B.3)}] \emph{The Stein factorisation.} Because $\sigma$ is a proper surjection with geometrically connected fibres (each fibre $\mathbb P^{h^0-1}_k$ is geometrically irreducible), its Stein factorisation
302	    \[
303	      C^{(g)} \xrightarrow{\;\sigma_*\;} \Spec_{\Pic^g_{C/k}}\bigl(\sigma_* \mathcal O_{C^{(g)}}\bigr) \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}
304	    \]
305	    presents the second arrow as a finite morphism whose source is the intermediate scheme. Since the fibres of $\sigma$ are connected, this intermediate scheme equals $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ scheme-theoretically (the Stein factorisation is trivial on the second arrow when fibres are connected, by Stein's classical theorem). The middle scheme is therefore $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ itself, which is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of dimension $g$. Translating by $-g P_0$ yields $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, and we take $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ as in Route A. The universal property is then established as in Sub-step A.4.
306	  \end{itemize}
307	
308	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route B.} Each sub-step is blocked by a different missing piece.
309	  \begin{itemize}
310	    \item Sub-step B.1 requires symmetric powers of schemes (the construction $\Sym^g$ for a $k$-scheme of finite type) and finite-group-scheme quotients of schemes (the quotient $C^g / S_g$). Mathlib has finite-group quotients of types and of various algebraic structures, but not yet a usable scheme-level quotient by a finite group action with the smooth-target property.
311	    \item Sub-step B.2 requires Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, including the relative version for families of curves --- the cohomology-and-base-change machinery for proper morphisms.
312	    \item Sub-step B.3 requires the Stein factorisation theorem for proper morphisms of locally Noetherian schemes ($f \colon X \to Y$ proper $\implies$ $f_* \mathcal O_X$ is a coherent $\mathcal O_Y$-algebra). Mathlib has partial coherence-theoretic infrastructure but does not yet have the cohomology of $\mathcal O$-modules of finite type along proper morphisms in the form needed.
313	  \end{itemize}
314	
315	  \paragraph{Genus-$0$ sub-case (absorbed into the same witness).}
316	  When $g(C) = 0$ the witness's underlying scheme is $J = \Spec k$, and the universal property reduces to a rigidity statement.
317	
318	  \begin{itemize}
319	    \item[\textbf{(C.1)}] \emph{Brauer--Severi identification.} A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ with $g(C) = 0$ and a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_k$. This is the Brauer--Severi triviality criterion: a Brauer--Severi variety of dimension $1$ is $\mathbb P^1$ over its base if and only if it has a rational point. The genus-$0$ identification on the curve side uses $h^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ (Riemann--Roch) to produce a degree-$1$ morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1_k$, which is then an isomorphism by degree-and-dimension.
320	
321	    \item[\textbf{(C.2)}] \emph{Rigidity for morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety.} We state the rigidity result over the algebraic closure $\bar k$, since the protected signature of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} does not assume $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ and the genus-$0$ identification $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ is only available after passing to $\bar k$ (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexamples to a $k$-side identification without a rational point). The conclusion over $k$ --- needed only in the sub-case $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, where Sub-step~C.1 gives $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$ already over $k$ --- then follows by Galois descent (Sub-step~C.2.f).
322	
323	    \begin{itemize}
324	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.a)}] \emph{Statement (over $\bar k$).} Let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$, with identity element $\eta_A \in A(\bar k)$. Let
325	      \[
326	        f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \longrightarrow A
327	      \]
328	      be a morphism of $\bar k$-schemes such that $f(p) = \eta_A$ for some closed point $p \in \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}(\bar k)$. Then $f$ factors as the composite $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A$; equivalently, $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$.
329	
330	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.b)}] \emph{Reduction to the project's rigidity lemma.} Let $c \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ denote the constant morphism at $\eta_A$ --- the unique $\bar k$-morphism factoring through the closed-point section $\eta_A \colon \Spec \bar k \hookrightarrow A$. The conclusion $f = c$ follows from Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} applied to $g_1 := f$, $g_2 := c$, $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, $Y := A$, once we produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $U.\iota \circ f = U.\iota \circ c$ as scheme morphisms. (After the iter-125 refactor, the cited theorem is stated at the scheme level: source reduced and geometrically irreducible, target separated; the source-side group-object decoration that the earlier formulation carried has been dropped. $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ satisfies the refactored hypotheses trivially --- it is smooth, hence reduced; it is geometrically irreducible over $\bar k$; and $A$ is proper hence separated over $\bar k$.) The reduction therefore amounts to: produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $f$ and $c$ agree as scheme morphisms; we will in fact take $U = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
331	
332	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.c)}] \emph{Image dimension argument.} The morphism $f$ goes from a proper geometrically integral $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $1$ to a smooth proper $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $g := \dim_{\bar k} A$. The set-theoretic image $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) \subseteq A$ is an irreducible closed subset of $A$ (the continuous image of an irreducible space under a proper map is irreducible and closed) of dimension at most $1$. If $g = 0$ then $A = \Spec \bar k$ and the conclusion is vacuous: the unique $\bar k$-morphism to $\Spec \bar k$ is the constant morphism. Henceforth assume $g \geq 1$, in which case the image is either a single point (the desired conclusion) or a one-dimensional irreducible proper closed subscheme of $A$ --- an irreducible rational curve in $A$.
333	
334	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Key classical input: proper rational curves on an abelian variety are constant.} The remaining case --- image of dimension exactly $1$ --- is ruled out by the following classical fact (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4, Proposition):
335	
336	      \begin{quote}
337	        \emph{Let $A$ be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$. Then every morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant.}
338	      \end{quote}
339	
340	      Two standard proofs are available; both unwind a few layers of upstream Mathlib infrastructure but are routine once the input lemmas are present.
341	      \begin{itemize}
342	        \item \emph{Via the dual abelian variety.} Pullback of line bundles along $f$ induces a homomorphism of group schemes $f^{\vee} \colon A^{\vee} \to \Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$, where $A^{\vee}$ is the dual abelian variety (the moduli space of degree-zero line bundles on $A$). The Picard group of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ has trivial degree-zero component, $\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0$, because every line bundle on $\mathbb P^1$ of degree $0$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}$ (Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6 and II.7). Hence $f^{\vee} = 0$. By autoduality of abelian varieties ($A^{\vee\vee} = A$ canonically, double duality of the Poincar\'e line bundle), the zero of $f^{\vee}$ implies that $f$ itself is constant.
343	
344	        \item \emph{Via the cotangent bundle.} The cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ of an abelian variety is a trivial $\mathcal O_A$-module: the tangent bundle of a group scheme over a field is generated globally by translates of the Lie algebra at the identity, hence is trivial, hence so is its dual. Pulling back along $f$ yields a morphism of locally free sheaves $f^{*} \Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}$, whose global sections form a $\bar k$-linear map
345	        \[
346	          H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}\bigr) \longrightarrow H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}\bigr) = H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(-2)\bigr) = 0.
347	        \]
348	        Triviality of $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ means the left-hand side is a non-zero $\bar k$-vector space (of dimension $g$); the only way the map can land in zero is for it to be the zero map at every fibre, i.e.\ for the differential $df$ to vanish identically on $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. Smoothness of $A$ over $\bar k$ together with the vanishing of $df$ forces $f$ to be a constant morphism on the geometric points and hence (by reduced-source / separated-target, see C.2.e) as a scheme morphism.
349	      \end{itemize}
350	      Either proof yields: $f$ is constant. The pointed condition $f(p) = \eta_A$ then identifies the constant value, so $f = c$ on underlying topological points.
351	
352	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promoting set-level equality to scheme equality.} The set-level equality $f = c$ on underlying topological points (established in C.2.d) promotes to scheme-morphism equality on any non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via the standard reduced-source / separated-target argument: $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is reduced (smooth implies regular, regular implies reduced) and $A$ is separated over $\bar k$ (proper implies separated), so a $\bar k$-morphism $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is determined by its values on the underlying topological space. In particular, $f|_U = c|_U$ as scheme morphisms for the open $U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ itself, and the reduction of C.2.b gives $f = c$ as scheme morphisms.
353	
354	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.f)}] \emph{[DROPPED iter-127.] Galois descent to $k$ --- no longer needed.} Prior strategy iterations routed the conclusion of C.2.d through the algebraic closure $\bar k$ and then descended morphism equality back to $k$ via faithful flatness of $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (M2.c in STRATEGY.md, $\sim 4$--$8$ iter / $300$--$500$ LOC). The iter-127 over-k analogist (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}) verified that the three active pieces of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile (cotangent triviality via the functorial shear iso $(a, b) \mapsto (a, a \cdot b)$; \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} as a $k$-agnostic Mathlib typeclass; characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$) all build directly over an arbitrary base field $k$ with no algebraic-closure or perfect-base hypothesis. The keystone of C.2.d is therefore established over $k$ directly, and \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (the project's named rigidity declaration; see \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}) is invoked in the $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch of \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} with the supplied $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$ feeding the pointing hypothesis. No base-change-and-descent step is performed. Galois descent of morphism equality is consequently \textbf{not} a Mathlib gap that the project needs to fill.
355	
356	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.g)}] \emph{Mathlib gap statement (iter-127 over-k inventory).} The keystone fact of Sub-step~C.2.d --- that every morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve over a base field $k$ to a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$ is constant --- is \emph{not} present in current Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) in any form usable here. The project records it as the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (the filename and Lean identifier still carry the iter-126 over-$\bar k$ name, but the signature is already $k$-agnostic --- \texttt{[Field kbar]}, no \texttt{[IsAlgClosed kbar]} --- and the prose framing has shifted to the over-k reading; a low-priority rename \texttt{rigidity\_over\_kbar} $\to$ \texttt{rigidity\_over\_k} is scheduled for iter-128+). The keystone decomposes into the shared cotangent-vanishing pile of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} pieces (i)--(iii): (i) cotangent triviality of a group scheme over an arbitrary base field $k$ via the functorial shear iso (no $\bar k$-rational-point lifting); (ii) scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec k$ via the $k$-agnostic \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass plus the iter-125-refactored \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}; (iii) characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$ (no perfect-base / alg-closure required). Each is an upstream Mathlib gap-fill in its own right (see \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}). When $g = \dim A = 0$ the statement is vacuous and the declaration's conclusion is automatic. The reduction step C.2.b reuses the project's already-formalised Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (after the iter-125 refactor, this is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, stated at the scheme level: source reduced and geometrically irreducible, target separated); the promotion step C.2.e uses standard reduced-source / separated-target lemmas, partially in Mathlib. The Galois-descent gap previously associated with C.2.f (M2.c and its companion M2.c.aux \texttt{geomIrred.exists\_kalg\_pt}) is \textbf{eliminated} under the iter-127 over-k commitment: rigidity is established directly over $k$, so no base-change-and-descent step appears anywhere in the M2 critical path. Total savings: $500$--$900$ LOC / $7$--$13$ iter relative to the prior over-$\bar k$-then-descend plan.
357	    \end{itemize}
358	
359	    \item[\textbf{(C.3)}] \emph{The trivial witness.} Take $J := \Spec k$, the terminal $k$-scheme. The group-object structure on $\Spec k$ is trivial; it is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of relative dimension $0 = g(C)$. The unique morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to \Spec k$ sends $P$ to the (unique) point. By Sub-step C.2 every $f \colon C \to A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ is constant at $\eta_A$, hence factors uniquely through the unique morphism $g \colon \Spec k \to A$ sending the point to $\eta_A$; uniqueness of $g$ follows because $\Spec k \to A$ is determined by its value on the unique $k$-rational point of $\Spec k$.
360	  \end{itemize}
361	
362	  \emph{Mathlib status for the genus-$0$ sub-case.}
363	  \begin{itemize}
364	    \item Sub-step C.1 requires the genus-$0$ identification on the curve side, which in turn requires Serre duality / the basic Riemann--Roch infrastructure that this project has explicitly trimmed (see the project's Phase~C exit policy). Mathlib has partial $\mathbb P^1$-theory but the curve-side genus-$0$ identification is missing.
365	    \item Sub-step C.2 reduces, via the project's already-formalised rigidity lemma (Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} of Chapter~\ref{chap:Rigidity}), to the classical fact that proper rational curves on a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over an arbitrary base field $k$ are constant (the keystone of Sub-step~C.2.d, packaged as \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}). This classical fact is not in Mathlib in the form needed here; see Sub-step~C.2.g for the explicit gap statement and the over-k inventory of pieces (i)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile. Galois descent of morphism equality is \textbf{no longer} a Mathlib prerequisite under the iter-127 over-k commitment: Sub-step~C.2.f is DROPPED (see above).
366	    \item Sub-step C.3 is unconditional once C.1 and C.2 are in place.
367	  \end{itemize}
368	
369	  \paragraph{Mathlib infrastructure summary.}
370	  Three independent Mathlib build-outs each unlock one route of the proof:
371	  \begin{itemize}
372	    \item[\textbf{($\alpha$)}] \emph{Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure plus FGA representability}: unlocks Route A. The single highest-cost build-out, since Hilbert/Quot schemes are upstream of much of modern algebraic geometry; but once present, the rest of Route A (identity component, smoothness, properness, universal property) follows by classical arguments already widely formalised in adjacent settings.
373	    \item[\textbf{($\beta$)}] \emph{Symmetric powers of schemes plus finite-group-scheme quotients plus Stein factorisation for proper morphisms (with its cohomology-of-$\mathcal O_X$-modules hypotheses)}: unlocks Route B. Each piece is a meaningful Mathlib contribution in its own right; the bottleneck is the smooth-quotient property of $C^g / S_g$.
374	    \item[\textbf{($\gamma$)}] \emph{The rigidity theorem for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, over an arbitrary base field $k$}: this is the prerequisite of the genus-$0$ sub-case under either Route A or Route B. The project packages it as \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; the Lean identifier still carries the iter-126 over-$\bar k$ filename but its signature is already $k$-agnostic), gated on the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: cotangent triviality of a group scheme over $k$ via the functorial shear iso; scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec k$ via the $k$-agnostic \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass; characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$. Per the iter-127 over-k commitment (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}), each piece builds directly over $k$ with no algebraic-closure, perfect-base, or Galois-descent prerequisite. The genus-$0$ arm of the witness existence is packaged as \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}), which invokes \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} directly with the supplied $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$ in the $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch and is vacuously true on the $C(k) = \emptyset$ branch (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$). Independently of ($\alpha$) and ($\beta$), establishing ($\gamma$) lets the genus-$0$ part of the witness be discharged unconditionally via \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}; the higher-genus part is packaged as \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness}, iter-134 scaffold) whose body closure remains gated on Route A ($\alpha$) or Route B ($\beta$). Per the iter-135 body restructure of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, the witness existence is now a case-split $\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0$ delegating to \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} respectively, converting the formerly inline \texttt{sorry} into honest delegation to the two named scaffolds.
375	  \end{itemize}
376	
377	  In sum: Mathlib currently contains none of the three infrastructure builds, and so the existence statement is recorded here as the project's single explicit foundational hypothesis. It backs all downstream consequences --- the four protected instances on $\Jac(C)$ (Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}), and the Abel--Jacobi map and its universal property of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} --- and is discharged by any one of the three routes above once the corresponding Mathlib infrastructure lands.
378	
379	  \paragraph{Iter-135 body restructure.} The Lean body of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} is, per iter-135 refactor, a case-split $\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0$ delegating to \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (genus-$0$ arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing iter-138+) and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} (positive-genus arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing M3 --- M3 is committed to Route A per the iter-144 disposition in STRATEGY.md \S~M3; sits behind M2 critical-path during the M2 wait window). The restructure converts the inline \texttt{sorry} on the witness-existence theorem itself into honest delegation to two named scaffolds; the witness existence is therefore conditionally established whenever both arms' bodies close (no Mathlib gap directly on the witness-existence theorem itself, only on each arm).
380	\end{proof}
381	
382	\subsection{The genus-$0$ arm of the witness existence}
383	\label{sec:genusZeroWitness}
384	
385	The genus-$0$ sub-case of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} admits an explicit witness construction at the Lean level, packaged separately from the general existence statement so that the genus-$0$ branch can be discharged independently once the shared cotangent-vanishing pile lands (\cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii)).
386	
387	\begin{theorem}\leanok
388	[Genus-$0$ Albanese witness]
389	  \label{def:genusZeroWitness}
390	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}
391	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, thm:rigidity_over_kbar, def:genus}
392	  Let $C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then there exists a $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C$ whose underlying scheme is $\Spec k$ (the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$), with the trivial group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension $0$, properness, and geometric irreducibility, and whose $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$ field encodes the Albanese universal property for every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$.
393	\end{theorem}
394	
395	\begin{proof}
396	  \uses{thm:rigidity_over_kbar, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, def:genus}
397	  \leanok
398	  We construct the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness}.
399	
400	  \paragraph{Underlying scheme.} Take $J := \mathbf{1}$, the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, which on underlying schemes is $\Spec k$ viewed as a $k$-scheme via the identity structure morphism.
401	
402	  \paragraph{Group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility.} The terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ of any cartesian-monoidal category carries the (unique) trivial group-object structure: multiplication and inverse are the unique morphisms to $\mathbf{1}$, and the identity element is the identity morphism. The structure morphism $\mathbf{1}.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}_{\Spec k}$ is proper (the identity is a closed immersion, in particular proper), smooth (the identity is unramified and flat, hence smooth), and geometrically irreducible. The geometric-irreducibility instance is the project-local helper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyIrreducible\_id\_Spec} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:120}--\texttt{126}), which derives geometric irreducibility of the identity on $\Spec k$ from the fact that, on the geometric fibre $\Spec K$ over any field extension $k \to K$, the second projection of the pullback square is an isomorphism, and $\Spec K$ is irreducible.
403	
404	  \paragraph{Smoothness of relative dimension $\genus\,C$.} The smoothness witness $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$ is required at relative dimension $\genus\,C$. The hypothesis $\genus\,C = 0$ rewrites the target relative dimension to $0$, after which the goal reduces to $\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 0\ (\mathrm{id}_{\Spec k})$. This is a Mathlib instance: the identity morphism on any base is smooth of relative dimension $0$.
405	
406	  \paragraph{The \texttt{isAlbaneseFor} field.} For every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$, we exhibit an \cref{def:IsAlbanese} term for the triple $(C, P, J)$.
407	
408	  The universal pointed morphism is the unique terminal map $\alpha := \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \colon C \to \mathbf{1}$ (the structure morphism of $C$ as a $k$-scheme, factored through the terminal object). The pointed condition $P \circ \alpha = \eta_J = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}$ holds automatically: both sides are morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, and the terminal object has a unique endomorphism.
409	
410	  For the universal factorisation: given a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with identity element $\eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$ and a morphism $f \colon C \to A$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, we must exhibit a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = \alpha \circ g$. \emph{Existence}: take $g := \eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$. Then $\alpha \circ g = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$, which equals $f$ precisely when $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. This is the content of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (the project's named rigidity declaration of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, established directly over the base field $k$ per the iter-127 over-k commitment): the supplied marked point $P \in C(k)$ feeds the pointing hypothesis, and the rigidity conclusion is exactly $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$ as scheme morphisms. \emph{Uniqueness}: any two morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to A$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ agreeing on $P \circ (-) = \eta_A$ are equal by the universal property of the terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ as a category (a morphism out of $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ is determined by where it sends the unique generator, here pinned to $\eta_A$).
411	
412	  \paragraph{Vacuity for the $C(k) = \emptyset$ branch.} When no $k$-rational point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$ exists (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexample), the universal quantifier $\forall (P : \mathbf{1} \to C), \mathtt{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J$ is vacuously satisfied at the Lean type level: Lean's $\forall$ over an empty type is trivially true. The construction handles this branch implicitly without any explicit case split.
413	
414	  \paragraph{Body closure status.} The Lean body of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness} (in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean}) is currently \texttt{sorry}; the closure is gated on the body of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (M2.a), which in turn depends on pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile (\cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}) landing in iter-129+. Earliest realistic body-closure iteration: iter-138+.
415	\end{proof}
416	
417	\subsection{The positive-genus arm of the witness existence}
418	\label{sec:positiveGenusWitness}
419	
420	The positive-genus sub-case of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} is packaged separately from the genus-$0$ arm so that the two arms can be closed independently. Whereas the genus-$0$ arm \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} routes through the rigidity declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, the positive-genus arm requires the full Albanese construction via Route A (Picard scheme via FGA --- Hilbert/Quot infrastructure plus FGA representability) of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, which is the committed M3 route per the iter-144 disposition in STRATEGY.md \S~M3. Route B (symmetric powers + Stein factorisation) is a historical alternative not pursued by the project.
421	
422	\begin{theorem}\leanok
423	[Positive-genus Albanese witness]
424	  \label{def:positiveGenusWitness}
425	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness}
426	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, def:genus, def:IsAlbanese}
427	  Let $C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with $\genus(C) \ge 1$. Then there exists a $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C$ whose underlying scheme is the Albanese variety of $C$ (classically the identity component of the Picard scheme $\mathrm{Pic}^0_{C/k}$, or equivalently the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism on $\mathrm{Sym}^g(C)$). The full $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}$ data (group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension $g$, properness, geometric irreducibility, and Albanese universal property uniformly over $k$-rational marked points) is supplied by the chosen construction (Route A or Route B per \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}).
428	\end{theorem}
429	
430	\begin{proof}
431	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
432	  \leanok
433	  This is the M3 arm of the witness existence. M3 is \textbf{committed to Route A --- Picard scheme via FGA} (per STRATEGY.md \S~M3 iter-144 disposition; $\sim 6500$ LOC midpoint per the iter-123 audit \texttt{analogies/m3-route-audit.md}). The autonomous loop writes the $\sim 6500$-LOC Mathlib material directly in-tree; upstream PR extraction is an OPTIONAL downstream lift, not a project deliverable. The committed route produces the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} in positive genus via the following Mathlib-prerequisite path:
434	  \begin{itemize}
435	    \item \emph{Route A: Hilbert/Quot infrastructure plus FGA representability of $\Pic_{C/k}$.} See \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route A for the four-step decomposition (A.1 relative Picard functor; A.2 representability; A.3 identity component; A.4 Abel--Jacobi universal morphism). The smallest entry point into Route A is the \texttt{RelativeSpec} functor (A.1 prerequisite; $\sim 700$--$1100$ LOC) per the iter-123 audit.
436	  \end{itemize}
437	  Route B (symmetric powers $C^{(g)} := C^g/S_g$ + Stein factorisation, $\sim 9000$ LOC midpoint per the iter-123 audit) is documented in \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route B as a historical alternative not pursued by the project.
438	
439	  M3 sits behind M2 critical-path during the M2 wait window; whether to start Route A scaffolding (smallest entry: \texttt{RelativeSpec} functor at $\sim 700$--$1100$ LOC) in parallel with M2 or strictly after M2 closes is a planner-level scheduling call. Existing iter-150 mathlib-analogist scheduling trigger: dispatched if cumulative M2.body-pile LOC exceeds $925$ LOC without piece (i.b) closing, OR if M2.a body closure has not landed by iter-160.
440	
441	  The scaffold \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness} (landed iter-134) is load-bearing iter-135 onward for the genus-stratified body restructure of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (iter-135 refactor): the body case-splits $\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0$ and delegates to \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} on the $\neg(\genus\,C = 0)$ arm via \texttt{Nat.pos\_of\_ne\_zero}. The Lean body of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness} is currently \texttt{sorry}; the closure is contingent on Route A of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} being prosecuted in full.
442	\end{proof}
443	
444	\section{Implementation route via the Albanese functor}
445	
446	Because the full Picard-scheme representability theorem is not available in current Mathlib, the formal construction proceeds in two layers:
447	
448	\begin{enumerate}
449	  \item \textbf{Layer I --- direct definition.} The Jacobian of $C$ is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, carrying the four properties of Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred} (each obtained by projection from the witness). The genus-$0$ case is included: the witness's universal property in that case carries the rigidity content for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme. Per the iter-127 over-k commitment (\texttt{analogies/cotangent-vanishing-pile-over-k.md}), this rigidity is established directly over the base field $k$ via the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} and the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii); no base-change to $\bar k$ and no Galois descent of morphism equality enters. The genus-$0$ arm is packaged as the explicit Lean construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}: the underlying scheme is the terminal object $\Spec k$, the universal morphism in the $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch is the unique terminal map \texttt{toUnit}\,$C$, and the universal factorisation reduces to \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}.
450	
451	  \item \textbf{Layer II --- Abel--Jacobi compatibility.} Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} introduces the Abel--Jacobi morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ and shows that it is universal among morphisms from $C$ to abelian varieties.
452	\end{enumerate}
453

Earliest realistic body-closure iteration: iter-138+.

input.old_string: \paragraph{Iter-135 body restructure.} The Lean body of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} is, per iter-135 refactor, a case-split $\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0$ delegating to \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (genus-$0$ arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing iter-138+) and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} (positive-genus arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing M3 --- M3 is committed to Route A per the iter-144 disposition in STRATEGY.md \S~M3; sits behind M2 critical-path during the M2 wait window). The restructure converts the inline \texttt{sorry} on the witness-existence theorem itself into honest delegation to two named scaffolds; the witness existence is therefore conditionally established whenever both arms' bodies close (no Mathlib gap directly on the witness-existence theorem itself, only on each arm).

input.content:
# Blueprint Writer Report

## Slug
jacobian-iter145

## Status
COMPLETE

## Target chapter
blueprint/src/chapters/Jacobian.tex

## Changes Made

### Edit 1 (MUST-FIX) — L369–L377 Mathlib infrastructure summary, route-disposition reconciliation
- **Revised** the `\paragraph{Mathlib infrastructure summary.}` block.
  - Replaced the opening "Three independent Mathlib build-outs each unlock one route of the proof" framing with: "Per the iter-144 M3 disposition (STRATEGY.md §~M3), the project commits to Route A for the positive-genus arm and treats Route B as a historical alternative not pursued. The Mathlib build-outs gating the two surviving arms are therefore:".
  - Item ($\alpha$) (Hilbert/Quot + FGA representability) now carries the parenthetical "(COMMITTED M3 infrastructure --- iter-144 disposition)" and is explicitly identified as the build-out that discharges the positive-genus arm.
  - Item ($\beta$) (symmetric powers + Stein factorisation) is now demarcated `($\beta$, historical only --- not pursued)`, kept as a brief paragraph cross-referencing Route B in `thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness`, and explicitly tagged as not part of the project's discharge path. The earlier route-unlocking framing for ($\beta$) is dropped.
  - Item ($\gamma$) (rigidity over $k$) wording is reworded: "prerequisite of the genus-$0$ sub-case under either Route A or Route B" → "prerequisite of the genus-$0$ sub-case under Route A"; "remains gated on Route A ($\alpha$) or Route B ($\beta$)" → "is gated on Route A ($\alpha$)"; "Independently of ($\alpha$) and ($\beta$)" → "Independently of ($\alpha$)".
  - Reordered so ($\beta$) appears last after ($\alpha$) and ($\gamma$) to visually emphasise that ($\beta$) is a historical entry attached at the end of an otherwise-two-item active list, rather than sitting between the two active items.
  - Updated the closing `In sum:` sentence: "Mathlib currently contains none of the three infrastructure builds" → "Mathlib currently contains neither of the two active infrastructure builds (($\alpha$) and ($\gamma$))"; "discharged by any one of the three routes" → "discharged once ($\alpha$) and ($\gamma$) both land"; appended a sentence clarifying ($\beta$) is preserved for scholarly context only.

### Edit 2 (MUST-FIX) — L425 `def:positiveGenusWitness` theorem statement
- **Revised** the trailing clause of the theorem statement.
  - Old: "supplied by the chosen construction (Route A or Route B per `\cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}`)".
  - New: "supplied by the Route A construction (per `\cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}` Route A; Route B is documented as a historical alternative not pursued)".
- The statement now matches the body's Route A-only commitment (the body at L433–L441 enumerates Route A as the committed route and explicitly tags Route B as the historical alternative not pursued).

### Edit 3 (SOON) — L414 `def:genusZeroWitness` body-closure iter estimate refresh + L379 companion cross-consistency
- **Revised** L414 body-closure status paragraph of `\def:genusZeroWitness`.
  - Replaced the trailing sentence "Earliest realistic body-closure iteration: iter-138+." with: an expanded sentence noting the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot trajectory (iter-146+ chart-algebra piece (ii) sub-piece prover lane; iter-149+ M2.a body closure (`thm:rigidity_over_kbar`); iter-151+ M2.b body closure (`def:genusZeroWitness`)) plus the revised earliest-realistic iteration "**iter-151+ (revised iter-145; under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot)**".
  - Also removed the stale phrase "landing in iter-129+" from earlier in the same sentence (the iter-129+ figure was inherited from the same stale slate as iter-138+ and would have remained inconsistent with the revised iter-146+ chart-algebra piece (ii) gate). Re-wording: "landing in iter-129+." is now part of the deleted suffix and is replaced by the chart-algebra-pivot trajectory sentence.
- **Revised** L379 `\paragraph{Iter-135 body restructure.}` for cross-consistency. The genus-$0$ arm marker "sorry body closing iter-138+" was updated to "sorry body closing iter-151+ under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot --- see `\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}` body closure status, revised iter-145". The positive-genus arm marker is unchanged (still: "sorry body closing M3 --- M3 is committed to Route A per the iter-144 disposition in STRATEGY.md §~M3; sits behind M2 critical-path during the M2 wait window").

## Cross-references introduced
- No new `\uses{...}` cross-references introduced (revisions are prose-only and stay within already-cited labels: `def:genusZeroWitness`, `def:positiveGenusWitness`, `thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness`, `thm:rigidity_over_kbar`, `sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile`).

## Macros needed (if any)
- None. All edits use existing macros and standard LaTeX constructs already in active use in the chapter.

## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
- None. The directive's edits are purely a reconciliation pass against the iter-144 disposition recorded elsewhere in the chapter (L286–L287 Route B header HISTORICAL, L420 positive-genus arm prose, L433–L441 positive-genus arm proof body). No external source material was needed.

## Notes for Plan Agent

- **Sanity-check on iter-numbering propagation.** The iter-144 chart-algebra pivot trajectory (iter-146+ piece (ii) → iter-149+ M2.a → iter-151+ M2.b) is sourced from the directive's "iter-145 Wave 2 chart-algebra envelope" framing. The numbers are inserted as written; the plan agent should verify they match the live trajectory in STRATEGY.md before iter-146 prover dispatches. If the live trajectory has shifted forward, both the L414 paragraph and the L379 companion sentence will need a refresh.
- **No `\leanok` / `\mathlibok` changes.** Per the writer's descriptor and the directive's "Boundaries" section, all three edits are prose-only. No marker was added or removed. The existing `\leanok` markers on `def:genusZeroWitness`, `def:positiveGenusWitness`, and `thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness` (statement and proof blocks both) are untouched.
- **Sibling chapter cross-consistency to monitor.** Did NOT verify (out of scope) whether sibling chapters (e.g. `AbelJacobi.tex`, `RigidityKbar.tex`, `Genus.tex`) reference the obsolete "iter-138+" genus-0 body-closure estimate or the "Route A or Route B" framing of `def:positiveGenusWitness`. If they do, those would benefit from a writer dispatch next iter (one writer per sibling chapter). The iter-145 blueprint-reviewer is the natural place to flag any such residual cross-chapter staleness.
- **L370 framing language.** I chose to keep the (α)/(β)/(γ) item labels rather than re-letter to drop (β). Rationale: the alphabet labels are referenced elsewhere in the chapter (notably the iter-127 over-k inventory in C.2.g and at L286–L287); re-lettering would have rippled through and exceeded the directive's scope. The (β) item is demoted in place to "historical only --- not pursued" instead.

## Strategy-modifying findings
None. The edits implement an already-committed strategic decision (the iter-144 Route A commitment on M3 with Route B dropped to historical alternative) and do not surface any new strategy-level concern.
